Bethlehem Township includes 18017 and 18020 zip codes and share one Township Municipal governing body. Since 2010, a
small part of 18020 was removed from District 135 and placed into District 138, which, from an administrative standpoint, makes
no sense. Until 2010, the entirety of 18020 was in District 135. The current State Rep for 138 has stated that it is difficult to figure
out which areas fall into 138--and the majority of that constituency is in a more rural area north of where I live, with different
business, recreational, and geographical issues. I am sure it is frustrating for Township officials as well. This was all part of a
gerrymandering scheme to garner more Republican votes, as District 135 has traditionally been center Left. In all the newsletters
of the previous and current 138 reps, never do I see any participation in 18020 activities. The 138 District office is nearly 10 miles
from this area, vs the 135 District office is 5 miles away and half the time to get there. It is also in an area that citizens of the
township are more likely to be traveling near as part of their daily lives. I would like to see all parts of 18020 restored to District
135.

Community Name:
PFAS - affected water areas in
Montgomery and Bucks County
Description:
Several townships in Bucks and Montgomery County have been affected by PFAS contamination of the water supply from the
previous Horsham/Warminster Air Base activity. This is a very important issue and grouping those townships together can help
make sure they get the focused support they need. The townships currently fall under those included in PA senatorial district 12.
There may be additional townships nearby that I am not aware of that have emerging water issues and that could be included.
The ones I am aware of include Hatfield, Montgomeryville, Horsham, Hatboro, Warrington and Warminster, and Upper Moreland.
Upper Dublin may also be affected - I think Ambler Borough is.

Community Name:
Centre Region Council of Governments
and State College Area School District
Description:
What's represented here are the boundaries of the six member municipalities that make up the Centre Region Council of
Governments (COG) (i.e. College, Ferguson, Halfmoon, Harris and Patton Townships, plus the Borough of State College). The area
shown also is effectively congruent with the State College Area School District (SCASD). Centre Region COG provides an
organizational structure to support municipal cooperation on regional issues including planning, development, codes, police
service, emergency services, other municipal services, transportation, parks, recreation, environment, water, waste management,
libraries, and other shared civic interests of the community. SCASD has similar regional reach, with multiple neighborhood
elementary schools, two consolidated middle schools, and a central high school campus. All serve the needs of regional youth
and their families via education, sports, recreation and other extra-curricular activities. Of course, Penn State's University Park
campus is centrally located within the COG region, providing significant educational, economic, recreational and social influences
on the surrounding community. Both COG and SCASD can serve as jurisdictional proxies for many of the civic and social
"communities of interest" that make up the Centre Region. Business patterns roughly follow, with many local residents, retailers,
service providers, contractors, and charitable groups working and doing business primarily within and across the Centre Region.
The region (plus some immediately adjacent townships) is loosely bounded geographically by ridges on either side. We all drink
water from the Spring Creek watershed. We use the same primary roads to traverse. exit, and re-enter and the region. In short,
the Center Region is a well defined and vibrant community on many dimensions - organizationally, economically, and culturally -
with a unique set of concerns to be represented by its elected representatives. It is a travesty that current congressional
districting splits the region in two and appends those fragments to geographically unrelated and largely rural districts that reach
out in broad swaths toward Bradford, to the NW, and to Susquehanna County, to the NE. On a personal note, I have been resident of the Center Region for well over 40 years. My wife and I both were students at Penn
State, where I also worked for over 30 years. Our daughters were born in Centre Community Hospital. Both attended and
graduated from State College Area schools. One attended Penn State and now lives locally in Patton Township with her husband
and our granddaughter. He teaches at Penn State and works more broadly with the Center Region business community. Our
granddaughter, will attend State College schools. They live within an 10 minute drive from my house. Yet I share a congressional
representative with my distant cousins in Montrose, while they share their representative with strangers in Bradford. That's not
right, and those involved with drawing the current maps know it. We can and should do much better this time around. The
Center Region's boundaries should be respected and its residents should share a common representative in Washington.
This area is strongly Democratic. It is important these places are represented in a Democratic leaning district.

A region of northern Dauphin (primarily) that is made up of the following school districts: Millersburg Area, Upper Dauphin,
Williams Valley, Line Mountain. Often times, Halifax Area is also included in this description (even though I didn't include them
here in this grouping). This area is like-minded, plays each other in sports, shops and does business in the same places, shares
family values, and is much more rural than the southern part of Dauphin County. It actually has much more in common with
Northumberland, Snyder and even Schuylkill counties than southern or central Dauphin county.

This area is where I live the majority of my life. It is where I shop, dine, attend events, recreate, receive my healthcare. It is where
my friends live.
